Introduction The global ocean has absorbed the excess heat due to human activities by more than 90% (IPCC, 2021), impact- ing the natural environment and socioeconomic system (Smith et al., 2021).
The increased ocean heat results in higher chances of discrete but unprecedentedly warm sea surface temperatures (SSTs), which are often accompa- nied by marine heatwaves (Oliver, 2019).

Using observational data and climate model simulations, recent studies have revealed that anthropogenic climate change has increased the severity and frequency of marine heatwaves over the past century globally (Frölicher et al., 2018; Laufkötter et al., 2020; Oliver et al., 2018; Pilo et al., 2019; Plecha & Soares, 2020) and in specific regions, such as the tropical Pacific (Holbrook et al., 2022), Northeast Pacific (Amaya et al., 2020; Barkhordarian et al., 2022), North Atlantic (Plecha et al., 2021), Indian Ocean (Saranya et al., 2022), Mediterranean Sea (Darmaraki et al., 2019), and East Asian marginal seas (Li et al., 2019; Miyama et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022; Yao et al., 2020).
